title: Banks’ new stress test as inflation, rates bite
full_text: Other Editions 29 April 2026 Business Banks’ new stress test as inflation, rates bite The Australian Eric Johnston, Associate editor 29 April 2026 1282 words English AUSTLN 21 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. As the economy is about to head into a rough patch, the bank regulator has put itself on high alert. In anticipation of another serious ­financial shock, the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority has launched a new wave of stress testing for the nation’s banks. The so-called “dynamic stress test” currently under way at APRA is designed to look at the reaction of the individual lenders in the face of the specific economic issues that are rising interest rates, inflation and a likely rise in unemployment. The move comes as the banks have started to top up the level of provisions on their balance sheets, adding to economic overlays. Those are lending losses not yet known but likely to emerge as the economy slows. Business-­focused National Australia Bank last week issued a sharp top-up in provisions, while Westpac has also added more protection to its balance sheet. A clear picture will emerge of just how the major banks are preparing for a sharp slowdown in coming weeks, with ANZ, NAB, Westpac and Macquarie scheduled to deliver their March half-year accounts. (Commonwealth Bank will deliver a quarterly update.) APRA’s dynamic stress test adds to two major tests the banks have already taken. There’s the major annual stress test it has performed since the Covid pandemic that is towards the end of the year that looks at the impact on the bank from a series of severe but plausible economic shocks. Then last year APRA finalised its first system-wide test that specifically looked at the interconnections between the big four banks and six biggest super funds and the associated risks these can present. While APRA characterises the banking system as sound, the preliminary results from that test have raised issues around liquidity risk, given the super funds represent a significant source of funding for banks. A drain on both super funds and banks at the same time will be a highly problematic issue for the nation’s financial system. The latest exercise is more around what APRA views as a “demanding near-term environment” for banks: rising interest rates, inflation and the additional domestic strains flowing from the Iran war. One way or another, these stresses either collectively or on their own are going to flow through to household and business cash flows, which ultimately affects the performance of a bank’s lending book. As part of the test, APRA is getting a more granular view of household versus business exposures, as well as commercial property exposures and where the vulnerabilities lie. So too is APRA alert to the ­recent grab for market share between banks on lending to small and mid-sized businesses. The competition among the big four for this slice of the market has been hot, given the higher returns on offer. There’s also the prospect that much business has been generated by brokers, which is increasingly proving to be a weak point around risk. Even so, the thinking at APRA is that the overall exposure to small and mid-sized businesses are within tolerances. Losses across one or two big institutional loans and large commercial properties could do far more ­damage. There’s no doubt the Iran war represents a new level of risk for the global financial system, with control of oil being used as leverage. The conflict has also exposed a new level of energy vulnerability for Australia. However, as a long-tail regulator, it’s less the causes of an economic shock that APRA is concerned about; rather, it’s the outcome of the event, particularly on banks. Virtual exercises like stress testing are designed to give APRA confidence around how big financial buffers are and how they perform through the cycle. They also become a useful tool in helping to uncover the unexpected. Nearly every bank crisis – from Silicon Valley Bank to the GFC – starts on the asset side, that is, with losses on the balance sheet. This leads to worries about asset quality and then spreads like wildfire into liquidity issues. Liquidity is the oxygen for banks; when it is cut off, the consequences are significant. There’s no suggestion Australia’s banks are sitting there with billions in loans that are about to turn bad, but the upcoming round of earnings results will be a chance to see if they are well protected in the case that the economy performs worse than expected. NAB set the tone for the banks last week when it increased the chances of a “bear case” scenario for the economy at 45 per cent. That’s almost a one-in-two chance. Among NAB’s assumptions for a bear case, the economy could shrink by as much as 2 per cent, unemployment could spike above 9 per cent next year, and house prices could go down sharply. As a result, it topped up its provisioning by $220m ahead of its first-half results, which now sit at $706m. It has $300m against collective provisions. Last month APRA chair John Lonsdale warned the risk of a drawn-out war or further escalation that could worsen the economic fallout is real and something the financial system must be prepared for. Kraken grows The Origin Energy-backed software platform Kraken has firmly watered down expectations of a stockmarket listing sometime this year, going with it hopes of a windfall for Origin shareholders of the London-based tech platform. Kraken is a specialist software platform that represents a one-stop shop for utilities that promises to do everything. This ranges from signing on new customers and billing as well as AI tools to manage dynamic pricing so households can theoretically get the best time-of-day deal. Origin’s near 23 per cent stake in Kraken is valued by investors at just over $4.6bn. In December the platform undertook a fundraiser, valuing it at more than $US8.6bn. This share restructuring got everyone excited; an initial public offering was coming this year. But Kraken CEO Amir Orad tells Australian investors that IPO plans for this year haven’t been mentioned and his team remains focused on what it is doing by signing up big utilities. After cracking the European market, Orad is focused on the US, where the energy players are big and the opportunities are plenty. The average utility in the US is running on multiple tech platforms that are about three decades old, Orad says. He’s also eyeing an expansion into water utilities where the software is even older. Still, Orad was keeping his cards close about the next leg of growth for Kraken, already past his own target of 90 million-plus customers using the platform. The reality is that the worry about the impact of AI on software-as-a-service players is real, and names like Kraken will get swept up in what’s been dubbed the “Saasacre”. Orad says there’s been zero shift in appetite for his business services since AI agents have been unleashed. He also says that, as the name implies, the Kraken platform gets into all parts of an energy company’s business and unlocks the data hidden away as far as AI agents are concerned. The entry of state-backed Saudi Energy as a minority shareholder last month didn’t affect the $US8.6bn valuation, he adds. Orad says no decisions have been made about an IPO and the business is being built up to operate at scale “as if we can one day be a public company”. johnstone@theaustralian.com.au The Australian-20260429-Australian-21-000500499190 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has initiated a new dynamic stress test for banks to assess their resilience amid rising inflation, interest rates, and potential unemployment increases. Major banks, including NAB and Westpac, are increasing their provisions for potential lending losses as they prepare for an economic slowdown. The results of these stress tests will provide insights into the banks' financial health and risk exposure ahead of their upcoming earnings reports.
publication:
Australia lacks national strategy to regulate AI spread in workplace, report states
Australian Broadcasting Corporation News28 April 20261,245 wordsnational work reporter Bronwyn Herb

Australia risks repeating the mistakes it made with social media unless it moves quickly to regulate the spread of artificial intelligence in the workplace, according to a new report.
key_points: APRA's dynamic stress test aims to evaluate banks' responses to economic challenges.
Banks are increasing provisions for potential losses as the economy slows.
Upcoming earnings reports will reveal banks' preparedness for a downturn.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights the importance of risk management and financial stability in the banking sector.
